text: An erroneous system state that can lead to unexpected system behavior is called:
explanation: A system error is an erroneous system state; faults are causes and failures are externally observed incorrect behavior.

text: Fault avoidance is the application of verification and validation techniques used to discover and remove faults in a system before it is deployed.
explanation: False. Discovering/removing faults via V&V is fault detection/removal; fault avoidance focuses on preventing faults from being introduced.

text: Reliability measures are system attributes that define the number of failures that are acceptable during normal use of the system.
explanation: False. Reliability measures quantify failure behavior (e.g., rate, probability), while acceptable failures are part of reliability requirements/specification.

text: ______ is the probability that the system will fail when a service request is made.
explanation: POFOD (Probability of Failure on Demand) is the probability of failure when a request/demand occurs.

text: Among the benefits of creating a reliability specification is:
options: It provides a basis for assessing when to stop testing a system
It reduces the cost of the system
It can be created after the system is implemented
explanation: A reliability specification provides a target to decide when testing is sufficient.

text: A specialized system associated with some other control system, which can take emergency action if a failure occurs, is called:
explanation: A protection system can take emergency action when a failure is detected.

text: The fault-tolerant solutions studied in class have as a core concept:
explanation: Fault tolerance typically relies on redundancy (extra components/paths) to continue operation despite faults.

text: Which is true of a self-monitoring system?
options: It should monitor its own operations and take action if inconsistencies are detected.
Extensive use should be made of diversity so hardware failure will not lead to each channel producing the same results.
explanation: Self-monitoring systems typically use multiple channels and self-checking; diversity may also be used to reduce common-mode failures.

text: An example of a validity check is range checking to determine if an input is acceptable.
explanation: True. Range checking is a standard input validity check.

text: A reasonable exception handling strategy is to:
options: Push the problem to the calling unit
Try to fix the problem in the handler
Pass the problem to a run-time support system
explanation: All three can be valid strategies depending on the failure context and system design.

text: In the C programming language, assuming int nums[100] is a valid declaration, nums[105]; may access memory outside the declared array bounds.
explanation: True. C typically does not perform bounds checking at runtime.

text: To recover from most post-delivery software faults it is usually enough to:
options: Throw exceptions and generate detailed error messages
Simply restart the program at a known stable point
Put the system in a safe state if an operational anomaly is detected
explanation: Many post-delivery faults are transient; restarting from a stable state often restores correct operation.

text: The idea behind checkpoint and restart systems is similar to the Memento design pattern.
explanation: True. Both involve saving internal state and restoring it later.

text: Programming for reliability includes:
options: Naming all real-world constants in programs
explanation: Array bounds checking and naming constants improve safety/clarity; heavy use of goto is generally discouraged.

text: Reliability testing is essentially the same as normal software testing.
explanation: False. Reliability testing focuses on measuring failure behavior under expected operational usage (often using an operational profile).

text: An operational profile defines:
options: A set of classes of expected inputs to a software system
The expected frequency of input classes
The time spent in individual operational modules
explanation: Operational profiles primarily define expected input/operation classes and their frequencies.

text: By far, the largest source of threats to software security results from vulnerability in:
explanation: Most exploitable vulnerabilities are found in application-level software and its dependencies.

text: Something of value which has to be protected is called:
explanation: An asset is anything valuable that needs protection.

text: Circumstances that have potential to cause loss or harm are called:
explanation: A threat is a potential cause of harm.

text: A weakness in a computer-based system that may be exploited to cause loss or harm is called:
explanation: A vulnerability is a weakness that can be exploited.

text: A threat that makes part of the system unavailable is:
explanation: Interruption affects availability (making systems/resources unavailable).

text: A threat that allows an attacker to gain access to an asset is:
explanation: Interception is unauthorized access (confidentiality breach).

text: Security requirements are classified into risk avoidance, risk detection and risk mitigation.
explanation: True. These are standard categories used for security requirements.

text: A misuse case describes how an unauthorized user of a system might attack it.
explanation: True. Misuse cases model attacker goals and attack paths.

text: A key to providing system security and reliability is creating an appropriate architecture.
explanation: True. Architecture choices strongly influence both reliability and security properties.

text: Software design should
options: Base decision on explicit security policy
Avoid a single point of failure
Specify the format of all system inputs
explanation: Secure software design should be based on explicit security policies, avoid single points of failure, and clearly specify system inputs. Therefore, all listed options are correct.

text: An issue with security testing is that security requirements are usually written as negatives and it is very difficult to prove something won’t happen.
explanation: Security requirements are often written as negatives (e.g., unauthorized access must not occur), and it is inherently difficult to prove that something will never happen.

text: Dependable programming guidelines for security issues are essentially the same as those for reliability.
explanation: Many dependable programming practices such as input validation, error handling, and defensive programming apply to both security and reliability.

text: The website of security issues shown in class was
explanation: Common Weakness Enumeration

text: The unified process model of software development is
options: A reasonable approach when requirements are well defined
A useful approach when a customer cannot define requirements clearly
The best approach to use for projects with large development teams
A risky model that rarely produces a meaningful product
explanation: The unified process (UP) is an iterative and incremental software development process that is particularly useful when requirements are not well defined at the outset, allowing for flexibility and adaptation as the project progresses.

text: A characteristic of a project is that a project
explanation: Projects are unique, have defined objectives and criteria, and consume resources. Therefore, all options are correct.

text: Software teams are formed based on which of the following factors?
options: The estimated size of the completed project
The language to be used in coding
The difficulty of the problem to be solved
explanation: Team formation depends on project size, technical requirements (including language), and problem complexity.

text: A product scope statement defines
options: Context of the product as determined by how it fits in existing systems
The information objectives as determined by customer-visible data or product architecture details
The function and performance of the product
explanation: A product scope statement defines the product context, objectives, functionality, and performance. Hence, all options are included.

text: Which is the correct flow of activities (left to right)?
options: Application domain -> design model -> analysis model -> solution domain
Solution domain -> design model -> analysis model -> Application domain
Application domain -> analysis model -> design model -> Solution domain
explanation: The correct progression moves from understanding the application domain, to analysis modeling, to design modeling, and finally to the solution domain.

text: V process model is essentially the waterfall process model with testing tied to each major activity.
explanation: The V-model extends the waterfall model by associating each development phase with a corresponding testing phase.

text: Among the factors that help projects succeed is
explanation: User involvement, experienced management, and clear requirements all contribute to project success.

text: Which diagram is not part of UML?
options: All of the above are UML diagrams
explanation: Data Flow Diagrams (DFD) are not part of UML; they belong to structured analysis methods.

text: Call and return architecture is based upon functions calling each other.
explanation: Call-and-return architecture is structured around hierarchical function calls.

text: A sequence diagram
options: Depicts functions that transform data flow
Indicates how data are transformed by the system
Indicates user-system interaction in a temporal relationship
explanation: A sequence diagram shows interactions between objects or actors over time, emphasizing temporal order.

text: “Structured analysis” views data and the processes that transform it as separate.
explanation: Structured analysis typically separates process modeling (e.g., DFDs) from data modeling (e.g., ER models).

text: The phases of the project management lifecycle that correspond to UP’s construction and transition phases are:
explanation: UP Construction aligns with Execution, and Transition aligns with Closing (deployment/rollout/hand-off).

text: In a UML sequence diagram, the arrows labeled by operations point to the class (object) that owns the operation.
explanation: Messages (operation calls) in a sequence diagram point to the receiver object; that receiver’s class owns/implements the operation.

text: A software project’s software configuration consists of:
explanation: Software configuration items include code, documentation, and data that must be versioned/controlled.

text: A “baseline” is a specification or product that has been formally reviewed and agreed upon, and thereafter serves as the basis for further development.
explanation: That is the standard SCM definition of a baseline.

text: A software configuration management repository is the set of mechanisms and data structures that allow a software team to manage change effectively.
explanation: An SCM repository provides storage and controls for versions, changes, and traceability.

text: Among a software configuration management repository’s features are:
explanation: Modern SCM repositories commonly support all of these capabilities.

text: Change control refers to the process of modifying elements before they are baselined.
explanation: Change control is primarily about controlling changes to baselined items via formal change requests/approval.

text: Among the reasons to collect data on software products and processes is to:
options: Gain understanding of processes and products
Establish baselines for comparisons in the future
Determine project status with respect to plans
Improve processes and products by identifying root causes and inefficiencies
explanation: All listed items are standard purposes of software measurement/metrics programs.

text: Which is not used to calculate Function Points (FPs)?
options: They are all used to calculate FPs
explanation: Traditional FP counting uses inputs/outputs/files (and related categories), not the number of potential use cases directly.

text: Function points are derived using an empirical relationship based on countable measures of software’s information domain and assessments of software complexity.
explanation: FPs are based on counted functional components plus adjustments reflecting general system characteristics.

text: Function point values are conditioned by weighting factor multipliers for “simple”, “average”, and “complex” difficulty.
explanation: FP counting weights the counted elements by complexity levels such as simple/average/complex.

text: Among the value adjustment factors for Function Points is:
options: Does the system require backup and recovery features?
Are there multiple users at any point in time?
explanation: Backup and recovery is a standard general system characteristic used as a value adjustment factor.

text: In OO metrics, the degree to which all operations work together to achieve a single, well-defined purpose is called:
explanation: Cohesion measures how strongly related and focused the responsibilities of a class/module are.

text: Physical source lines are the length of code as it appears on paper or screen.
explanation: Physical SLOC counts the actual lines as written (including formatting/blank/comment policies depending on definition).

text: Logical source lines are the length of code as it appears on paper or screen.
explanation: Logical SLOC counts executable statements/logic (independent of formatting on the screen/paper).

text: The goal of software project planning is to:
options: Gather requirements, design software modules and implement code
Control, track and monitor a complex technical project
explanation: Project planning focuses on organizing and managing the work—tracking, controlling, and monitoring the project.

text: The software scope describes, in part, the functions and features that are to be delivered to end users.
explanation: Scope includes major functions/features, boundaries, and what will be delivered.

text: Among the ways to define software scope is:
options: Create a software architecture before coding commences
Estimate the number of person-months to complete the project
Develop a set of use cases created by end users
Determine project status with respect to plans
explanation: Use cases are a common method for defining scope from the user’s perspective.

text: Assume productivity is 800 LOC/person-month and size is 30,000 LOC. How many person-months will this project take?
explanation: Person-months = 30,000 / 800 = 37.5.

text: Using the above (37.5 person-months) and labor rate $12,152/month, what is the estimated cost?
options: Cannot be determined from the information given
explanation: Cost = 37.5 * 12,152 = $455,700, which is not listed, so 'None of the above'.

text: The cost per LOC on this project is $15.19.
explanation: Cost/LOC = 455,700 / 30,000 = $15.19 exactly, so it is True; therefore the statement is True (note: this makes the correct answer True).

text: Given count-total = 320 and ΣFi = 30, FP = count-total * (0.65 + 0.01 * ΣFi). What is FP?
If average productivity is 6.0 FP/person-month, how many person-months will the project last (rounded up)?
explanation: Person-months = 304 / 6 = 50.67, rounded up = 51, so the correct option is 51 (option d).

text: Total cost = avg productivity * labor rate per FP * person-months. What is total cost?
explanation: Using the provided formula: 6.0 * 1200 * 51 = $367,200, which is not listed, so 'None of the above'.

text: Using function decomposition techniques (like counting processes in a DFD) for estimation purposes leads to highly accurate and detailed estimates.
explanation: Decomposition can improve detail, but estimates are still uncertain; 'highly accurate' is generally not true.

text: A critical path is a chain of tasks that determines the duration of a project.
explanation: The critical path is the longest-duration path through the task network and sets the project duration.

text: What COCOMO II model is used during the earliest stages of software engineering?
explanation: COCOMO II uses the Application Composition model for earliest/rough estimation (e.g., object points).

text: Project risk components are categorized by:
explanation: Risk components are commonly categorized by performance, cost, support, and schedule.

text: Risk exposure is computed as:
options: The probability that a risk will occur
The cost to the project if the risk should occur
The product of a and b
explanation: Risk exposure = Probability × Loss (impact/cost).

text: Among reasons projects are delivered late is:
options: An unrealistic deadline established by someone outside the software development group
Changing customer requirements that are not reflected in schedule changes
An honest underestimate of the effort and/or resources required
explanation: All listed factors commonly contribute to late delivery.

text: The most reasonable order for the project scheduling process is: estimate activity resources, allocate people, identify activities, find activity dependencies.
explanation: Reasonable order is identify activities → determine dependencies → estimate resources/duration → allocate people.

text: Project activities (tasks):
options: Have a duration in days, weeks or months of calendar time
Need an estimation of effort in person-days or person-months
explanation: Tasks are basic planning units with durations and effort/resource estimates.

text: A WBS is a hierarchical description of all the activities scheduled for a project.
explanation: A WBS (Work Breakdown Structure) is a hierarchical description of all the deliverables/work products — not scheduled activities.

text: A task network is a graphical representation of task flow for a project used to set up task sequences and dependencies.
explanation: Task networks (PERT/CPM) show task ordering and dependencies.

text: Which is not a Scheduling Principle?
explanation: Compartmentalization and interdependency determination are scheduling principles; risk analysis is a project risk activity (often related, but not a core scheduling principle list item).

text: The reason adding people to a late project makes it later is because the new person drains time from other project participants to get him/her up to speed.
explanation: This reflects Brooks’ Law: adding manpower to a late software project makes it later due to ramp-up and communication overhead.

text: In EVA, budgeted cost of work performed (BCWP) is the value of:
options: All work tasks that should have been completed by a point on the project schedule
All work tasks that have actually been completed by a point on the project schedule
The sum of the effort actually expended on work tasks completed by a point in time
explanation: BCWP (Earned Value) represents the budgeted value of the work actually completed.

text: Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT) and Critical Path Method (CPM) do completely different tasks in software project scheduling.
explanation: PERT and CPM are closely related network scheduling techniques with similar purposes.

text: A recommended distribution of effort across front-end, construction, and testing activities is:
explanation: A common guideline is roughly 40% front-end, 22% construction, and 40% testing.

text: According to Boehm’s model of cost changes, the relative cost of correcting an error during field operations is how many times more costly than during requirements?
explanation: Boehm’s curve suggests field correction can cost up to 1000 times more than fixing during requirements.

text: According to Lehman, S-type programs are those that model real-world processes and become part of the real world by their use.
explanation: That describes E-type systems; S-type programs are formally specifiable mathematical solutions.

text: Adding new pieces of hardware to an existing system usually necessitates what type of maintenance?
explanation: Adaptive maintenance modifies software to operate in a changed environment (e.g., new hardware).

text: Problem (bug) reports:
options: Are most helpful when they contain information on how to reproduce the bug
Should have only one bug per report
explanation: Effective bug reports are documented, reproducible, and contain one issue per report.

text: Among the legal reasons to reverse engineer someone else's software is:
options: To achieve interoperability and develop competing software
To evaluate software quality and robustness
To learn the steps of an algorithm
explanation: Interoperability and evaluation can be legal in some contexts; copying algorithm details directly may violate IP.

text: The reverse engineering case that started the PC clone industry was IBM vs Phoenix.
explanation: Phoenix successfully reverse-engineered the BIOS; it was not IBM winning against Phoenix.

text: The most important goal of an obfuscation technique is that it should be maximally stealthy (hidden from the reverse engineer’s knowledge).
explanation: Effective obfuscation should make reverse engineering difficult and ideally not obvious.

text: An anti-reverse engineering technique is removing symbolic information from compiled code.
explanation: Stripping symbols makes reverse engineering more difficult.

text: Control transformation as an obfuscation technique should alter program semantics (behavior).
explanation: Obfuscation must preserve semantics while altering structure.

text: A watermark in a program or media file is a secret message that can be used to prove ownership.
explanation: Digital watermarking embeds ownership information.

text: Static program comprehension is based on analyzing source code structure.
explanation: Static analysis examines code without executing it.

text: Program comprehension is what maintenance programmers spend most of their time doing.
explanation: A large portion of maintenance effort is spent understanding existing code.

text: Which mental model relies on programmers grouping several statements to understand what the code is doing?
explanation: Chunks refer to grouping statements into meaningful units for understanding.

text: One key factor in scaling up program comprehension is:
options: The use of parsing technologies for automatic code analysis
explanation: Automated parsing and analysis tools help scale comprehension for large systems.

text: Lehman’s first law says that programs in a real-world environment must change or become less useful.
explanation: This is the Law of Continuing Change.

text: About 60% of faults are found by rigorous testing after implementation.
explanation: Most defects are found earlier through inspections and reviews rather than only post-implementation testing.

text: Grace Hopper invented the term “bug” to describe a program defect.
explanation: The term existed earlier; she popularized it after documenting a moth in a relay.

text: Which is not a recognized process model flow?
explanation: Common flows include linear, iterative, and evolutionary; transformational is not a standard flow category.

text: Extreme Programming (XP) is a use-case driven, architecture-centric, iterative and incremental process closely aligned with UML.
explanation: That description matches the Unified Process, not XP.

text: Of the 'four P’s' of a project, process is the most important element of a successful project.
explanation: All four P’s (People, Product, Process, Project) are important; people are often considered the most critical.

text: Boundary classes are used to create a user interface to software.
explanation: Boundary classes model interactions between the system and external actors (often UI components).

text: Regardless of stakeholder type, all share the same set of concerns for a project.
explanation: Different stakeholders (customer, manager, developer) have different concerns and priorities.

text: Formal interpersonal procedures for team coordination focus on quality assurance activities applied to software engineering work products.
explanation: Formal coordination mechanisms such as reviews and audits are tied to QA of work products.

text: DFDs are useful in representing which analysis model element?
explanation: Data Flow Diagrams represent flow-based elements of the analysis model.

text: Which is not a process model umbrella activity?
explanation: Use case modeling is a modeling activity, not an umbrella activity.

text: Which is not a process model umbrella activity?
explanation: Modeling is a framework activity, not an umbrella activity.

text: IEEE standard 1058.1 defines the phases of project management.
explanation: IEEE 1058 addresses Software Project Management Plans.

text: Which of the following is a typical operation performed by a class?
explanation: Class operations may manipulate data, compute values, query state, or monitor events.

text: In a UML class diagram aggregation is represented by a black diamond pointing to the aggregating class.
explanation: Aggregation is shown with a hollow (white) diamond; black diamond represents composition.

text: The 'Blob' anti-pattern describes an OO structure where one class contains almost all functional elements and others are mere data holders.
explanation: That is the definition of the Blob (God class) anti-pattern.

text: A functional requirement is anything you can write code to achieve.
explanation: Functional requirements describe system behavior; not everything coded is necessarily a requirement.

text: A data-centered architecture is:
options: An older form of program architecture
Requires careful data management to avoid corruption
explanation: All statements apply to data-centered architectures.

text: What is not visible in a class diagram?
explanation: Detailed behavior (algorithmic logic) is not shown in class diagrams.

text: Analysis classes are determined by underlining each noun or noun phrase and entering it into a table.
explanation: Noun phrase analysis is a common technique for identifying candidate analysis classes.

text: All use case actors must be human.
explanation: Actors can be other systems or devices, not only humans.

text: A user story represents a feature or function of software used by an actor.
explanation: User stories describe functionality from the user's perspective.

text: Entity classes are derived from the project’s application domain.
explanation: Entity classes model domain concepts.

text: In a local version control system, it is possible to share files in the repository.
explanation: Local VCS repositories are not inherently shared across users.

text: In a centralized version control system:
options: A single server contains all versioned files
Only one client at a time can check out files
explanation: Centralized systems use a single server repository accessible by clients.

text: In Git, ______ means diverging from the main line of development and starting a new line of development.
explanation: Branching creates a separate line of development.

text: A good quality for a metric is to be simple and computable.
explanation: Good metrics should be objective, consistent, and easy to compute.

text: A(n) ______ is a quantitative measure of the degree to which a system possesses a given attribute.
explanation: A metric is a quantitative measure of a system attribute.

text: In OO metrics, an indirect implication about the degree to which a class can be reused refers to:
explanation: Completeness

text: One of the most important factors in estimating cost and schedule is:
options: The experience level of the estimator
explanation: Experience and historical data are critical for accurate estimation.

text: One way to estimate the size of a software project is to rely on expert judgment.
explanation: Expert judgment is a common size estimation technique, especially early in a project.

text: Process measurement is done indirectly by measuring the outcomes of the process itself.
explanation: Process quality is often inferred indirectly through product and outcome measures.

text: One of the goals of tracking process metrics is:
options: To identify problem employees and retrain them
explanation: Process metrics are intended to improve organizational productivity, not to single out individuals.

text: A typical project metric counts effort per software engineering task.
explanation: Effort per task is a common project metric.

text: Project metrics should:
options: Measure the resources consumed in the project
Count the deliverables created in the project
explanation: Effective project metrics include resource use, outputs, and effectiveness.

text: A useful project metric for an OO system is:
options: Number of support classes per analysis class
explanation: Only a and b are specific to OO systems; FP per person-month is a general productivity metric.

text: A danger of underestimating a project is that the team will likely have difficulty meeting shortened deadlines.
explanation: Underestimation often leads to unrealistic schedules and deadline pressure.

text: Process-based estimation approximates time for activities based on historical productivity and sums them to compute total effort.
explanation: Process-based estimation relies on historical data tied to process activities.

text: Bottom-up estimating based on a WBS:
options: Is a flawed technique requiring expert judgment
Can produce accurate results if activities are well understood
explanation: Bottom-up estimating can be accurate but is time-consuming.

text: In most estimating techniques, estimate accuracy is affected by:
options: Estimator’s ability to determine product size
Ability to translate size into person-months and cost
explanation: All listed factors influence estimation accuracy.

text: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) is:
options: DRE = E/(E+D), where E = errors found after delivery and D = defects found before delivery
DRE = E/(E+D), where E = errors found before delivery and D = defects found after delivery
explanation: DRE measures defects found before delivery divided by total defects.

text: One view of requirements that considers data and processes as connected entities is called:
explanation: OOA integrates data and behavior (processes) within objects.

text: An ______ is a metric or combination of metrics that provides insight into a process, project, or product.
explanation: An indicator interprets one or more metrics to provide insight.

text: Which is not a process model umbrella activity?
options: All of the above are umbrella activities
explanation: Monitoring and control is a framework activity, not an umbrella activity.

text: The optimal development time (in terms of cost) for a project is not the earliest possible time.
explanation: Compressing schedules increases cost due to added resources and inefficiencies.

text: In EVA, BCWS represents:
options: The total amount of work in the entire project
The total amount of work accomplished at a point in time
The total amount of work planned for a single task at a point in time
explanation: BCWS (Planned Value) represents the planned work scheduled at a specific time.

text: Project scheduling takes place ______ project estimates.
explanation: Scheduling is based on completed project estimates.

text: Analysis classes differ from data objects primarily in:
options: Classes can have operations and data objects cannot
Data objects can have operations and classes cannot
explanation: Analysis classes encapsulate both data and operations.

text: PERT is a technique for determining:
options: The critical path for a project
Cost associated with a software engineering task
explanation: PERT is used to analyze task sequencing and identify the critical path.

text: Earned value analysis:
options: Uses known data from previous projects
explanation: Both a and b are correct; EVA computes progress and can use historical data for comparison.

text: A critical path is a list of tasks that can be started simultaneously.
explanation: Critical path is the longest dependent chain of tasks determining project duration.

text: An important factor in estimation for OO projects is:
options: Determine the number of key classes
explanation: Key classes are a critical factor in estimating OO projects, as they often drive complexity and effort.

text: The 'make or buy decision' is supported by determining cost.
explanation: Cost comparison is central to make-or-buy decisions.

text: Which is not a risk due to business impact?
options: Are CASE tools used for analysis, design and testing
They are all risks due to business impact
explanation: Use of CASE tools is a technical risk, not directly a business impact risk.

text: WBS means:
explanation: WBS stands for Work Breakdown Structure.

text: The “risk table” is sorted by risk probability and risk occurrence impact.
explanation: Risk tables prioritize risks based on probability and impact (risk exposure).

text: Corrective maintenance is aimed at adding missing functionality.
explanation: Corrective maintenance fixes defects; adding functionality is perfective maintenance.

text: Fixing potential Y2K problems is what type of maintenance?
explanation: Y2K fixes were preventive maintenance to avoid future failure.

text: Fixing a fault causing inaccurate results is what type of maintenance?
explanation: Corrective maintenance fixes discovered defects.

text: Adding the ability to sort on any field is what type of maintenance?
explanation: Adding new features is perfective maintenance.

text: Modifying software to use a new bar code reader is what type of maintenance?
explanation: Adaptive maintenance adapts software to new environments or hardware.

text: Developing new software costs more than maintaining old software.
explanation: Maintenance often consumes more total lifecycle cost than initial development.

text: If we wrote perfect programs free of faults, we would never need maintenance.
explanation: Maintenance is also required for enhancements and environmental changes.

text: The Y2K issue resulted from COBOL programs that used 2-digit year fields.
explanation: Two-digit year storage caused ambiguity in year 2000.

text: Courts have consistently ruled that reverse engineering of copyrighted software is:
options: Legal if the reverse engineering does not use any of the code
Legal no matter how much original code is used
explanation: Reverse engineering is generally legal for interoperability if no copyrighted code is copied.

text: One issue with disassembly of binary code is:
options: There’s too much symbolic information about variables
There’s no symbolic information about variables
It’s impossible to tell the difference between subprograms
explanation: Disassembly loses symbolic information and structure clarity.

text: Refactoring is a type of reverse engineering that relies on disassembly of binary files.
explanation: Refactoring restructures source code without changing behavior.

text: Refactoring should be done on code that 'smells'.
explanation: Code smells indicate poor design needing refactoring.

text: A misuse case describes how an authorized user misuses the system.
explanation: Misuse cases often describe malicious or unauthorized misuse.

text: A good quality for a metric is to be programming language independent.
explanation: Metrics should ideally be language independent.

text: A metric’s attributes should be programming language independent.
explanation: Language independence improves general applicability.

text: The critical path for this project is:
If we remove the dependency for T3 on T2 and let T3 start earlier, the project will take 12 days assuming two people per task.
explanation: True, removing the dependency allows T3 to start at day 0, so T1 (2 days) + max(T2 (4 days), T3 (6 days)) + T4 (2 days) = 12 days.

text: UML use case diagrams are useful in representing which analysis model elements?
explanation: Use case diagrams represent scenarios and interactions.

text: Cohesion is a qualitative indication of the degree to which a module:
options: Completes its function in a timely manner
explanation: High cohesion means a module focuses on a single responsibility.

text: Coupling is a qualitative indication of the degree to which a module:
options: Is connected to other modules and the outside world
Mon acroglisseur est plein des anguilles!
explanation: Coupling measures interdependence between modules.

text: Horizontal partitioning defines separate branches of module hierarchy for each major function.
explanation: Horizontal partitioning separates major functions into branches.

text: The most common layered architecture is called:
explanation: Three-tier architecture is a common layered style.

text: An architectural style encompasses:
explanation: Architectural styles define components, constraints, and semantics.

text: Data that outlasts the current execution of a program is called:
explanation: Persistent data survives beyond a single program execution.

text: Before an architectural pattern can be chosen for a system it must have a code implementation to facilitate reuse.
explanation: Architectural patterns describe structure; they do not require an existing code implementation.

text: A threat that allows an attacker to insert false information into a system is:
explanation: Fabrication involves inserting counterfeit data or transactions.

text: Which of these is not an element of a requirements model?
explanation: Requirements models typically include scenario, class-based, and behavioral elements.

text: Among the typical means used to gather requirements are:
explanation: All listed methods are common requirements elicitation techniques.

text: Which of the following is not part of a CRC card?
explanation: CRC stands for Class, Responsibility, Collaboration.

text: The concept of data abstraction is similar to the creation of:
explanation: Data abstraction models data as objects with defined properties.

text: A(n) ______ is the overall structure of a software system.
explanation: Architecture defines the system’s overall structure.

text: Which is not an architectural style?
explanation: Throw & Catch is not a recognized architectural style; it is a programming construct.

text: Structured analysis views data and processes as separate.
explanation: Structured analysis separates data and process modeling.

text: The 5 generic software engineering framework activities are communication, planning, sequencing, construction and integration.
explanation: The correct activities are communication, planning, modeling, construction, and deployment.

text: The V process model is essentially the waterfall model with testing tied to each major process.
explanation: The V-model associates development stages with corresponding testing stages.

text: Iterative model of software development is:
options: Reasonable when requirements are well defined
explanation: Iterative models are effective when requirements evolve.

text: In traditional requirements gathering, interviewing stakeholders is the primary technique used.
explanation: Stakeholder interviews are a primary elicitation technique.

text: Which are recognized process flow types?
explanation: All listed are recognized process flow types.

text: Typically a use case may have multiple success and failure scenarios.
explanation: Use cases often include alternate success and failure paths.

text: Waterfall model flaws included:
options: Design gap between analysis and design
explanation: All listed are known waterfall limitations.

text: The Unified Process is an agile process aligned with UML.
explanation: Unified Process is iterative but not classified as agile.

text: Use case and use case diagram are the same concept.
explanation: A use case is a narrative; a use case diagram is a visual representation.

text: A Scrum sprint involves:
explanation: A sprint is a time-boxed iteration delivering backlog items.

text: A benefit of using CRC cards is:
explanation: CRC cards are flexible, collaborative modeling tools.

text: Which are part of agility?
explanation: All listed elements are core agile values.

text: Cost of change in agile development is typically less than in traditional models.
explanation: Agile reduces cost of change through iterative delivery.

text: Constant user involvement is viewed as a liability in agile methods.
explanation: Agile considers customer involvement essential.

text: During project inception the intent is to determine:
explanation: Inception focuses on understanding scope and vision.

text: Refactoring is an example of ______ maintenance.
explanation: Refactoring improves internal quality to prevent future problems.

text: The Y2K disaster was caused by using two-digit year fields in COBOL.
explanation: Two-digit year representation caused ambiguity.

text: Which is not a problem associated with maintenance?
explanation: Having accurate documentation is beneficial, not a problem.

text: Software maintenance is the ______ phase of a lifecycle.
explanation: Maintenance consumes the largest portion of lifecycle cost.

text: Which maintenance type is largest percentage of effort?
explanation: Perfective maintenance (enhancements) is typically the largest.

text: In peer-to-peer networks, there is no central server controlling communication.
explanation: P2P networks are decentralized.

text: The term 'bug' was coined because a moth crashed an early computer.
explanation: The term existed earlier; the moth incident popularized it.

text: In practice, most software architectures are:
explanation: Most real-world systems use mixed architectural styles.

text: Control transformation as an obfuscation technique:
explanation: Control transformation preserves semantics, often uses opaque predicates, and may introduce additional execution paths.

text: Static program comprehension:
options: Is based on analyzing source code structure
Is based on observed program behaviour
Looks at execution traces and call graphs
explanation: Static comprehension analyzes source code without executing it.

text: Which type of maintenance deals with problems arising from new hardware and software interfaces?
explanation: Adaptive maintenance modifies software to operate in new environments.

text: Which one is not a product metric?
explanation: McCabe cyclomatic complexity and Function Points are classic product metrics; the duplicated 'Hanson Index' entry is not typically treated as a standard product metric in this context.

text: Scheduling resources for a software project would not be possible without:
options: Understanding the language requirements for coding
Every task having a defined outcome
A project being compartmentalized into management tasks
explanation: Resource scheduling depends on task definition and decomposition; language/technology constraints can also affect staffing.

text: Assume productivity is 800 LOC/person-month and size is 30,000 LOC. How many person-months will this project take?
explanation: Person-months = 30,000 / 800 = 37.5.

text: What is the estimated cost of the project?
explanation: Using the given labor rate $12,152/month and 37.5 person-months: cost = 37.5 × 12,152 = $455,700, which is not listed.

text: A generic software development process model needs:
options: Framework, umbrella activities, framework activities and task sets
Iteration to make sure all requirements are captured
Good debugging to remove all errors during development
explanation: A generic process framework is defined by framework activities, umbrella activities, and task sets.

text: A data object consists of:
explanation: A data object includes the entity name and its data attributes; operations are typically part of classes, not data objects.

text: Which is not one of the 4P’s of a software project?
explanation: The 4P’s are People, Product, Process, and Project (not Planning).

text: In a software project the role of the customer is to specify the requirements for the software to be engineered.
explanation: Customers/stakeholders define requirements and needs for the system.

text: Which is not true in XP?
options: The customer writes the user stories
XP teams have only two members
XP is a linear process model
XP is an Agile Process Model
explanation: XP is iterative/incremental and agile, not a linear process model.

text: The probability of a failure-free system operation over a specified time in a given environment for a given purpose is called:
explanation: Reliability is the probability of failure-free operation for a specified time under stated conditions.

text: A characteristic of a software system that can lead to a system error is:
explanation: A system fault is a defect that can cause an erroneous system state (error).

text: Reliability specifications provide a basis for assessing when to stop testing a system.
explanation: Reliability specifications can define target reliability levels that guide stopping criteria.

text: Verification and validation techniques are used to identify and remove faults in a system before deployment.
explanation: V&V primarily supports fault detection/removal before release.

text: The following are true about availability: (a) measures fraction of time available, (b) takes repair/restart time into account, (c) relevant for non-stop running systems.
explanation: Availability concerns uptime fraction, includes repair/restart, and matters for continuously running systems.

text: The following are true about reliability specification: (a) specs can be incomplete/incorrect so a conforming system may still 'fail' for users, (b) users often don't read specs, (c) perceived reliability matters in practice.
explanation: All three points are commonly cited issues with reliability specifications.

text: _____ metric is relevant for systems where the system must process a large number of similar requests in a short time.
explanation: ROCOF is useful for systems handling many requests/transactions over time.

text: Which security design guideline advises that systems should continue protecting sensitive information even during failure?
options: Avoid a single point of failure
explanation: Fail securely means security properties are preserved even when failures occur.

text: The most appropriate metric for measuring the reliability of an insulin pump is Rate of Occurrence of Failure (ROCOF).
explanation: For demand-based safety-critical devices, probability of failure on demand is typically more appropriate than ROCOF.

text: 92% of reported vulnerabilities are in applications, not in networks.
explanation: The statement is marked as true in the provided exam image.

text: In the preliminary risk assessment process, which step involves decomposing threats into specific ways in which an attack might occur?
explanation: Attack assessment breaks threats into concrete attack scenarios/paths.

text: Which method in security validation involves simulating real attacks to try to breach the system?
explanation: Penetration testing simulates realistic attacks to find exploitable weaknesses.

text: Which of the following is a risk detection requirement?
options: Designing the system so that the risk cannot arise
Ensuring the system can recover after an attack
Identifying risks when they occur and neutralizing them
Encrypting patient data on a client device
explanation: Risk detection focuses on detecting/recognizing risks/events and responding to neutralize them.

text: According to the slides, which entity is responsible for reviewing and approving changes to baselines?
explanation: The CCB is responsible for evaluating and approving baseline changes.

text: Which of the following is not one of the four basic software configuration management (SCM) processes?
explanation: Core SCM processes include identification, version control/change control, status accounting, and audits/reviews—not risk assessment.

text: A software configuration item (SCI) is defined as:
options: Any hardware part of the system
A collection of software elements treated as a unit
explanation: An SCI is any set of software elements managed as a single unit under configuration control.

text: A company migrates from Windows Server 2012 to Windows Server 2022, requiring updates to database drivers and API calls. This is:
explanation: Adaptive maintenance adapts software to changes in its environment (OS/platform/APIs).

text: In distributed version control systems (DVCS), clients store only the latest snapshot of the repository.
explanation: DVCS clients typically have the full repository history, not just the latest snapshot.

text: In Git, the term committed means:
options: The file is staged but not stored
The data is safely stored in the local database
The changes were pushed to a remote server
The file was modified but not saved
explanation: A commit records changes in the local repository database/history.

text: Y2K-like Date Issue: Engineers proactively update code months before a system-wide timestamp change. This is:
explanation: Preventive maintenance is done to prevent future failures before they occur.

text: Developers refactor a large module to reduce complexity and improve readability before future enhancements. This is:
explanation: Refactoring to improve maintainability/quality (for future change) is often categorized as perfective maintenance in this exam context.

text: Customers request a new feature that automatically emails monthly reports. Developers implement the feature and update documentation. This is:
explanation: Adding new user-requested functionality is perfective maintenance.

text: Program slicing helps identify the parts of code that influence a specific variable or computation.
explanation: Program slicing isolates statements affecting a chosen variable/output.

text: When to reengineer a system: (a) changes confined to part → reengineer that part, (b) hardware/software support becomes obsolete, (c) tools to support restructuring are available.
explanation: Reengineering is often triggered by obsolescence and availability of restructuring support/tools (as marked).

text: An array of actions designed to both eliminate symbolic information and confuse program structure is called:
explanation: Obfuscation intentionally makes code harder to understand and reverse engineer.

text: IsDebuggerPresent API can detect user-mode debuggers.
explanation: IsDebuggerPresent can indicate whether a debugger is attached to the current process.

text: Some anti-debugger techniques intentionally execute operations that can disrupt or disable a debugger.
explanation: Anti-debugging can include tactics that interfere with debugging tools.

text: The cost of developing new software is often significantly higher than the cost of reengineering software.
explanation: Reengineering can be cheaper than greenfield redevelopment in many cases.

text: Risk exposure is computed as:
options: The probability that a risk will occur
The cost to the project if the risk should occur
The product of a and b
explanation: Risk exposure = Probability × Impact (loss).

text: Project risk components are categorized by:
explanation: Risk components are commonly grouped as performance, cost, support, and schedule.

text: Risks may be categorized as: (a) Intolerable, (b) ALARP, (c) Acceptable.
explanation: All three are common risk categories in risk assessment (intolerable/ALARP/acceptable).

text: The following are risks related to business impact, except:
options: The effect of this product on the company's revenue
Visibility of this product by senior management
Whether the customer has a solid idea of requirements
Number of customers who will use this product
explanation: Customer clarity of requirements is typically a project/process risk rather than a direct business impact risk.

text: The following are risks related to process maturity, except:
options: Whether a common process framework has been established
Whether project teams consistently follow the defined process
Whether management supports software engineering practices
Whether formal technical reviews are conducted
Whether the product has strong market demand
explanation: Market demand is a business/product risk, not a process maturity risk.

text: Problems with re-structuring are:
explanation: Restructuring can lose comments/docs and may require significant computational effort.

text: Reasons to reverse engineer include: (a) interoperability with proprietary software, (b) developing competing software, (c) evaluating software quality and robustness.
explanation: All listed items are presented as reasons to reverse engineer in the exam image.
